analysis:
1. 2028 Democratic Nomination: A Fragmented, Leaderless Field

The Democratic nomination market remains historically diffuse, with no candidate commanding meaningful probability. Michelle Obama leads the field at just 1.3%, followed by Cory Booker at 1.2% and Zohran Mamdani at 1.1% — a spread so compressed it reflects genuine uncertainty rather than preference. The field includes celebrities (Oprah at 0.8%, MrBeast at 1.0%, LeBron James at 1.0%), politicians (Roy Cooper at 0.9%, Gina Raimondo at 1.0%), and legacy names (Hillary Clinton at 0.8%, Bernie Sanders at 0.8%), all priced essentially at noise levels. The market is functioning as a long-horizon placeholder rather than a predictive instrument.

2. 2028 Republican Nomination: Equally Undifferentiated, With Musk as Outlier

The Republican field shows the same price compression, with Elon Musk at 1.1% holding the nomination lead — despite being constitutionally ineligible for the presidency. Matt Gaetz (1.1%), Tom Brady (0.9%), Greg Abbott (1.0%), and Rand Paul (1.0%) cluster tightly below. Sarah Huckabee Sanders at 0.7% and John Thune at 0.6% sit at the bottom of the tracked field. The market assigns no candidate a meaningful edge, with the entire Republican nomination probability spread spanning only 0.5 percentage points from top to bottom.

3. Celebrity and Longshot Candidates: High Volume, Near-Zero Probability

Several of the highest-volume markets in the dataset are dedicated to candidates with near-zero electoral credibility. LeBron James ($35.4M volume, 0.7%), Chelsea Clinton ($42M volume, 0.8%), and George Clooney ($34M volume, 0.8%) are among the most-traded markets despite sub-1% prices. Kim Kardashian is priced on both parties — 0.8% Democratic, 0.7% Republican — reflecting speculative recreational trading rather than informed probability assessment. These markets are volume leaders likely due to novelty appeal, not genuine forecasting activity.

4. General Election 2028: No Dominant Frontrunner Anywhere

General election markets mirror nomination chaos. Elon Musk leads all general election candidates at 1.2%, followed by Tulsi Gabbard (1.0%), Stephen Smith (1.0%), Nikki Haley (1.0%), and Greg Abbott (0.9%). Glenn Youngkin sits at 0.8%, Vivek Ramaswamy at 0.8%, and LeBron James at 0.7%. No candidate in either the nomination or general election markets breaches 1.5%, indicating that with 2.5 years until the election, the market sees the race as genuinely open with no consensus heir apparent on either side.

5. Key Shifts This Week

John Fetterman posted the largest weekly gain among tracked candidates, rising +0.4pp to 1.0% on $15M volume — a notable move for a candidate at this price level. Greg Abbott's Republican nomination odds gained +0.3pp to 1.0%, and Glenn Youngkin's general election price also rose +0.3pp to 0.8%, suggesting modest accumulation of interest in the Republican governor tier. Sarah Huckabee Sanders is the week's clearest loser, dropping -0.3pp to 0.7% — the only candidate showing meaningful downward movement. Zohran Mamdani gained +0.2pp to 1.1% on the Democratic side, making him the highest-priced Democratic nominee candidate this week alongside Cory Booker (+0.2pp to 1.2%). All moves remain within fractional percentage point ranges, but the directional signal favors second-tier governors and progressive longshots over establishment names.
